_meta.source: EC Regulation No 1333/2008 on Food Additives
_meta.notes: Positive list system: additives NOT in this list are banned by default in EU food

e102.name: Tartrazine (Yellow 5)
e102.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e104.name: Quinoline Yellow
e104.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e110.name: Sunset Yellow FCF (Yellow 6)
e110.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e120.name: Cochineal / Carminic Acid
e120.notes: Must be labeled as 'carmine' or 'cochineal extract'

e122.name: Azorubine / Carmoisine
e122.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e124.name: Ponceau 4R (Cochineal Red A)
e124.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e129.name: Allura Red AC (Red 40)
e129.notes: Must carry label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

e171.name: Titanium Dioxide
e171.notes: Banned in EU food since August 7, 2022 (genotoxic concerns). Still used in US food products.

e173.name: Aluminium
e173.notes: Only for surface decoration of sugar-coated products

e216.name: Propyl para-hydroxybenzoate (Propylparaben)
e216.notes: Banned in EU food since 2015 (endocrine disruption concerns). Still used in some US products.

e239.name: Hexamethylene tetramine
e239.notes: Banned in EU food. Only approved for Provolone cheese in some EU countries historically.

e301.name: Sodium ascorbate
e301.notes: Extended June 2025 (Regulation EU 2025/1150): now authorized as antioxidant in microencapsulated vitamin A preparations for infant formula and follow-on formula.

e304.name: Fatty acid esters of ascorbic acid

e310.name: Propyl gallate
e310.notes: Limited to fats, oils and chewing gum only in EU; broader use in US

e319.name: tert-Butylhydroquinone (TBHQ)
e319.notes: Not authorized in EU food. Commonly used in US processed foods and fast food oils.

e320.name: Butylated Hydroxyanisole (BHA)
e320.notes: Limited to specific food categories and max levels in EU. Under review. Broadly used in US.

e321.name: Butylated Hydroxytoluene (BHT)
e321.notes: Limited to specific food categories in EU. Broadly used in US cereals and packaging.

e341i.name: Monocalcium phosphate
e341i.notes: Used as a leavening acid in baking powder and self-raising flour. Reacts with sodium bicarbonate (E500) to produce CO₂. Also acts as an acidity regulator and mineral supplement. Permitted in the EU under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

e407.name: Carrageenan
e407.notes: Approved in EU; banned in organic EU products. Under ongoing EFSA review.

e410.name: Locust bean gum
e410.notes: Generally approved. Feb 2026 (Regulation EU 2026/196): maximum level reduced to 5,300 mg/kg in dietary foods for infants and young children for special medical purposes (categories 13.1.5.1 and 13.1.5.2).

e412.name: Guar gum
e412.notes: Generally approved. Feb 2026 (Regulation EU 2026/196): authorization REVOKED in infant formulae (category 13.1.1) and dietary foods for infants for special medical purposes (category 1.5.1) — production/import must cease by August 18, 2026. Still permitted at 10,000 mg/kg in category 13.1.5.2 (dietary foods for infants 4+ months for special medical purposes) until April 27, 2027.

e440.name: Pectins
e440.notes: Generally approved. Feb 2026 (Regulation EU 2026/196): maximum level reduced to 4,000 mg/kg in dietary foods for infants and young children for special medical purposes (categories 13.1.5.1 and 13.1.5.2).

e471.name: Mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids

e472a.name: Acetic acid esters of mono- and diglycerides

e472b.name: Lactic acid esters of mono- and diglycerides

e472c.name: Citric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides

e472e.name: Mono- and diacetyl tartaric acid esters (DATEM)

e500ii.name: Sodium hydrogen carbonate
e500ii.notes: Common baking soda. Releases CO₂ when heated or reacts with acid (e.g. E341i), causing dough to rise. Also used as an acidity regulator and anti-caking agent. Widely regarded as safe; one of the most commonly used food additives worldwide. Permitted in the EU under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

e904.name: Shellac
e904.notes: Jan 2026 (Regulation EU 2026/189): authorization extended to dietary foods for special medical purposes (category 13.2), specifically tablets and coated tablet formats.

e927a.name: Azodicarbonamide
e927a.notes: Not authorized as food additive in EU. Used as dough conditioner in US bread products (including fast food buns). Banned in EU and Australia.

e928.name: Benzoyl peroxide
e928.notes: Not authorized in EU flour treatment for food use.

e930.name: Calcium peroxide
e930.notes: Not authorized for flour treatment in EU food.

e951.name: Aspartame
e951.notes: Must carry warning for phenylketonurics: 'contains a source of phenylalanine'

e952.name: Cyclamates
e952.notes: Banned in US but approved in EU

e999.name: Quillaia extract
e999.notes: Oct 2025 (Regulation EU 2025/2084): updated authorized uses and compositional specifications. Effective July 1, 2026. Used as a foaming agent in soft drinks.

e1450.name: Starch sodium octenyl succinate (OSA starch)
e1450.notes: Feb 2026 (Regulation EU 2026/196): maximum level reduced to 10,000 mg/kg in dietary foods for infants and young children for special medical purposes (categories 13.1.5.1 and 13.1.5.2), compliance deadline February 18, 2028. Must not contain gluten in infant and follow-on formulae. Stricter microbiological criteria and residual sulphur dioxide limit (10 mg/kg dry basis) now apply.

e103.name: Alkannin
e103.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B (Union list of authorised colours) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e105.name: Fast Yellow AB
e105.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e106.name: Riboflavin-5-sodium phosphate
e106.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. The EU-authorised riboflavin phosphate form is listed separately as E 101(ii) Riboflavin-5'-phosphate sodium; E106 as a distinct number is not on the EU Union list.

e107.name: Yellow 2G
e107.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour (also banned in several other countries).

e111.name: Orange GGN
e111.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e121.name: Citrus Red 2
e121.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e125.name: Scarlet GN
e125.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e126.name: Ponceau 6R
e126.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e128.name: Red 2G
e128.notes: Suspended/removed from the EU Union list of authorised colours after an EFSA opinion (5 July 2007) found it is metabolised to aniline, a suspected genotoxic carcinogen. Formally withdrawn by Commission Regulation (EC) No 884/2007.

e130.name: Indanthrene Blue RS
e130.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e142.name: Green S
e142.notes: Authorised in the EU (Annex II Part B) but only for a limited list of food categories with maximum levels (e.g. certain desserts, sauces, tinned/preserved peas ('mushy peas'), confectionery, edible ices) — not permitted for general/unrestricted use.

e143.name: Fast Green FCF
e143.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not authorised as a food colour in the EU (it is FDA-approved in the US but was never carried onto the EU Union list).

e150.name: Caramel
e150.notes: Umbrella term covering the four EU-authorised caramel colour subtypes: E150a Plain caramel, E150b Caustic sulphite caramel, E150c Ammonia caramel, E150d Sulphite ammonia caramel — all listed in Annex II Part B, each with its own permitted food categories/maximum levels (some quantum satis, some capped, especially E150c/d).

e152.name: Black 7984
e152.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour (historical colour dropped during the 1994 EU colour harmonisation).

e154.name: Brown FK
e154.notes: Removed from the EU-wide Union list; its only historical use (colouring smoked/cured herring, i.e. 'kippers') was a domestic UK derogation, not an EU-wide authorisation, and is not part of the current EU Annex II colours list.

e155.name: Brown HT
e155.notes: Authorised in the EU (Annex II Part B) but only for a narrow set of food categories with maximum levels (notably chocolate/cocoa cakes and some other specific categories) — not for general use. Not one of the six 'Southampton' azo dyes that trigger the mandatory child-attention warning label.

e160e.name: Beta-apo-8'-carotenal (C30)
e160e.notes: Listed in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 with permitted food categories and maximum levels.

e160f.name: Ethyl ester of beta-apo-8'-carotenic acid (C30)
e160f.notes: Not carried onto the Union list of food additives when it was established — Commission Regulation (EU) No 1129/2011 (11 Nov 2011) excluded it because no business operator supported its re-evaluation/continued manufacture; it is not currently EU-authorised for food use.

e161i.name: Citranaxanthin
e161i.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — not authorised as an EU food colour (used only as a poultry-feed pigmenter under separate feed-additive rules, not as a food additive).

e161j.name: Astaxanthin
e161j.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — not authorised as an EU food colour additive. Astaxanthin is separately permitted as a feed additive for farmed salmon/trout/shellfish, and certain astaxanthin sources have separate novel-food/supplement authorisations, but as a direct food colour (E161j) it is not on the Union list.

e161a.name: Flavoxanthin
e161a.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — dropped from the EU colours list during the 1994 harmonisation (Directive 94/36/EC) and not carried into the current Union list.

e161c.name: Cryptoxanthin
e161c.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — dropped from the EU colours list during the 1994 harmonisation and not carried into the current Union list.

e161d.name: Rubixanthin
e161d.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — dropped from the EU colours list during the 1994 harmonisation and not carried into the current Union list.

e161e.name: Violaxanthin
e161e.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — dropped from the EU colours list during the 1994 harmonisation and not carried into the current Union list.

e161f.name: Rhodoxanthin
e161f.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — dropped from the EU colours list during the 1994 harmonisation and not carried into the current Union list.

e161g.name: Canthaxanthin
e161g.notes: Authorised in the EU (Annex II Part B) but only for one narrow traditional use (specific sausage products, e.g. 'Saucisse de Strasbourg') with a strict maximum level, following EFSA safety concerns (2010/2013) about crystalline deposits forming in the human retina at higher intakes. Not permitted for general food use.

e161h.name: Zeaxanthin
e161h.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — not authorised as an EU food colour additive.

e163a.name: Cyanidin
e163a.notes: Not separately listed; this specific anthocyanin pigment is covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, which is permitted only in specific food categories (quantum satis in some, maximum levels in others) rather than for general use.

e163b.name: Delphinidin
e163b.notes: Not separately listed; covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, permitted only in specific food categories rather than for general use.

e163c.name: Malvidin
e163c.notes: Not separately listed; covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, permitted only in specific food categories rather than for general use.

e163d.name: Pelargonidin
e163d.notes: Not separately listed; covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, permitted only in specific food categories rather than for general use.

e163e.name: Peonidin
e163e.notes: Not separately listed; covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, permitted only in specific food categories rather than for general use.

e163f.name: Petunidin
e163f.notes: Not separately listed; covered by the single authorised entry 'E163 Anthocyanins' in Annex II Part B, permitted only in specific food categories rather than for general use.

e164.name: Saffron
e164.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B as an authorised colour additive. (Saffron used as the traditional spice/foodstuff itself does not require additive authorisation, but as an isolated colouring-extract additive it is not on the EU Union list.)

e165.name: Gardenia Blue
e165.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — not an EU-authorised food colour.

e166.name: Sandalwood
e166.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B — historical colour dropped during the 1994 EU colour harmonisation and not carried into the current Union list.

e180.name: Litholrubine BK
e180.notes: Authorised in the EU (Annex II Part B) but exclusively for surface colouring of hard and semi-hard cheese rind, not intended for consumption — one of the narrowest-use colours on the list, not permitted in any other food.

e181.name: Tannin
e181.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B (colours) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 as a food colour additive. Tannins/tannic acid do have separate, narrow EU authorisations as processing aids (e.g. fining/clarifying agents in wine and beer under Regulation (EC) No 606/2009), but that is a different legal basis from being an authorised food colour additive under 1333/2008.

e182.name: Orcein
e182.notes: Not present in Annex II Part B of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 — not an EU-authorised food colour. Secondary sources indicate it has not been permitted for food colouring use since the 1970s, but an exact EU legal-instrument date could not be confirmed.

e201.name: Sodium sorbate
e201.notes: Not on the EU Union list of authorised additives; the sorbate group's twin salt calcium sorbate (E203) was formally deleted from Annex II/III by Commission Regulation (EU) 2018/98 due to insufficient genotoxicity data, and sodium sorbate has not been separately authorised.

e203.name: Calcium sorbate
e203.notes: Deleted from the EU Union list of authorised food additives (Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008) by Commission Regulation (EU) 2018/98 of 22 January 2018, because in the absence of adequate genotoxicity data its continued inclusion could not be justified.

e209.name: Heptylparaben
e209.notes: Never part of the EU Annex II list; only methyl and ethyl parabens and their sodium salts (E214-E219) are EU-authorised preservatives. Heptylparaben is banned as a food additive in the EU (also banned in Switzerland and Canada; permitted only in the USA).

e233.name: Thiabendazole
e233.notes: Not authorised as a food additive under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008; thiabendazole is used only as a post-harvest fungicide/pesticide, regulated separately under EU pesticide maximum-residue-level rules (Regulation (EC) No 396/2005), not as an Annex II additive.

e236.name: Formic acid
e236.notes: Reported removed from the EU list of authorised food additives (formerly used in fish, fruit and vegetable products); not present in the current Annex II.

e237.name: Sodium formiate
e237.notes: Authorised in the EU only in a very limited number of specifically approved food categories under Annex II conditions of use; not a general-purpose preservative and rarely appears on labels.

e238.name: Calcium formiate
e238.notes: Not authorised as a food additive for human food in the EU; calcium formate is used in the EU only as an animal feed preservative, which is a separate regulatory regime from Annex II food additives.

e240.name: Formaldehyde
e240.notes: Removed from the EU list of authorised food additives; not present in current Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

e242.name: Dimethyl dicarbonate
e242.notes: Authorised in the EU only as a preservative in certain beverages (still/non-alcoholic drinks, wine, cider, beer-mix drinks, etc.) at a maximum level of 250 mg/L; it fully decomposes during processing so is not detectable in the final product.

e243.name: Ethyl lauroyl arginate
e243.notes: Authorised in the EU only for heat-treated meat products, at a maximum level of 160 mg/kg (as ethyl lauroyl arginate), following Commission Regulation (EU) No 506/2014 and subsequent EFSA re-evaluations.

e246.name: Glycolipids
e246.notes: Newly authorised EU-wide by Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/1037 of 29 June 2022, inserted into Annex II Part B; permitted only as a preservative in beverage categories 14.1.4, 14.1.5.5 and 14.2.1.

e265.name: Dehydroacetic acid
e265.notes: Not confirmed present in the current EU Annex II list (also absent from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list). Dehydroacetic acid/sodium dehydroacetate appear to be authorised only as cosmetic preservatives under a separate EU annex, not as food additives.

e266.name: Sodium dehydroacetate
e266.notes: Not approved as a food additive in the EU; absent from Annex II and from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list. Authorised only as a cosmetic preservative under separate EU cosmetics legislation.

e267.name: Buffered vinegar
e267.notes: Newly authorised EU-wide by Commission Regulation (EU) 2023/2086 (applicable from 19 October 2023); permitted only as a preservative/acidity regulator in specific food categories including mozzarella and whey cheese, canned/bottled fruit and vegetables, fresh (pre-cooked) pasta, certain breads and meat preparations.

e303.name: Potassium ascorbate
e303.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II; absent from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list. Only ascorbic acid (E300), sodium ascorbate (E301), calcium ascorbate (E302) and ascorbyl esters (E304) are EU-authorised in this group; potassium ascorbate is authorised in Australia/New Zealand but not in the EU/UK.

e307a.name: D-Alpha-tocopherol
e307a.notes: Corresponds to EU-authorised alpha-tocopherol (E307), permitted generally (quantum satis) as an antioxidant.

e307b.name: concentrated tocopherol
e307b.notes: Corresponds to the EU-authorised tocopherol-rich extract / mixed tocopherols concentrate (E306), permitted generally as an antioxidant.

e307c.name: DL-Alpha-tocopherol
e307c.notes: Synthetic alpha-tocopherol, corresponding to EU-authorised E307, permitted generally as an antioxidant.

e313.name: Ethyl gallate
e313.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or in the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only propyl gallate (E310) is the EU-authorised gallate ester antioxidant.

e317.name: Erythorbin acid
e317.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only erythorbic acid (E315) and sodium erythorbate (E316) are EU-authorised in this group.

e318.name: Calcium erythorbin
e318.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only erythorbic acid (E315) and sodium erythorbate (E316) are EU-authorised in this group.

e322a.name: Oat lecithin
e322a.notes: EU-authorised since Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/1023 of 28 June 2022, restricted to use as an emulsifier in cocoa and chocolate products (as covered by Directive 2000/36/EC) at a maximum level of 20,000 mg/kg.

e323.name: Anoxomer
e323.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list. This niche non-digestible polymeric antioxidant does not appear to have genuine EU authorisation, despite low-quality web sources claiming otherwise; it was excluded from most jurisdictions' additive lists globally around 2010.

e324.name: Ethoxyquin
e324.notes: Never authorised as a food additive for human consumption in the EU. Also explicitly denied authorisation as an animal-feed antioxidant by Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2017/962 (7 June 2017) after EFSA raised safety and data-gap concerns, including a possible mutagenic impurity (p-phenetidine).

e325.name: sodium lactate
e325.notes: General-purpose EU-authorised additive, permitted quantum satis in most food categories.

e326.name: potassium lactate
e326.notes: General-purpose EU-authorised additive, permitted quantum satis in most food categories.

e327.name: calcium lactate
e327.notes: General-purpose EU-authorised additive, permitted quantum satis in most food categories.

e328.name: ammonium lactate
e328.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only sodium, potassium and calcium lactate (E325-E327) are EU-authorised in this group.

e329.name: Magnesium lactate
e329.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only sodium, potassium and calcium lactate (E325-E327) are EU-authorised in this group.

e342.name: Ammonium phosphate
e342.notes: Not confirmed as an authorised general Annex II food additive (absent from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list). Ammonium phosphates are used in EU winemaking as a yeast nutrient under separate oenological-practice rules, which is a different regime from Annex II food additive authorisation.

e343.name: Magnesium phosphates
e343.notes: EU-authorised (quantum satis in permitted categories) covering monomagnesium and dimagnesium phosphate.

e344.name: Lecithin citrate
e344.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e345.name: Magnesium citrate
e345.notes: Not confirmed as an authorised Annex II food additive (absent from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list). Magnesium citrate is used in the EU as a mineral source in food supplements under separate legislation (Directive 2002/46/EC), not as a general Annex II additive.

e349.name: ammonium malate
e349.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e353.name: Metatartaric acid
e353.notes: EU-authorised only for use in wine, per Annex II conditions specific to wine products.

e354.name: Calcium tartrate
e354.notes: EU-authorised, restricted to use in wine.

e359.name: ammonium adipate
e359.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list; only adipic acid and its sodium/potassium salts (E355-E357) are EU-authorised in this group.

e365.name: Sodium fumarate
e365.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list (fumaric acid itself, E297, is authorised, but the sodium/potassium/calcium/ammonium fumarate salts E365-E368 are not confirmed).

e366.name: Potassium fumarate
e366.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e367.name: Calcium fumarate
e367.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e368.name: ammonium fumarate
e368.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e370.name: 1,4-Heptonolactone
e370.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e375.name: Nicotinic acid
e375.notes: Not confirmed as an authorised Annex II food additive (absent from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list). Nicotinic acid/niacin is used in the EU as a vitamin ingredient in food supplements and fortified foods under separate legislation, not as a general Annex II food additive.

e380.name: Triammonium citrate
e380.notes: EU-authorised (quantum satis in permitted categories) per Annex II.

e381.name: ferric ammonium citrate
e381.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e383.name: calcium glycerophosphate
e383.notes: Not confirmed present in current Annex II or the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e385.name: Calcium disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate
e385.notes: EU-authorised only in a narrow set of specific food categories (e.g. canned pulses/legumes, canned crustaceans, dressings and sauces, certain canned/bottled foods) at low maximum levels; not approved for general use.

e388.name: Thiodipropionic acid
e388.notes: Not authorised as an EU food additive; absent from current Annex II and from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e391.name: Phytic acid
e391.notes: Not authorised as an EU food additive; absent from current Annex II and from the UK's retained-EU-law approved additives list.

e392.name: Extracts of rosemary
e392.notes: EU-authorised antioxidant restricted to specific food categories, with maximum levels expressed as carnosic acid + carnosol (commonly in the range of roughly 12-400 mg/kg depending on food category) per Annex II as amended by Commission Regulation (EU) No 1129/2011 and subsequent acts.

e408.name: Baker's yeast glycan
e408.notes: Not part of the EU Union list (Annex II to Reg. 1333/2008). This is a US/FDA-authorised additive (21 CFR 172.898) that was never adopted into EU food additive law; not found in the UK retained-EU additives register either.

e409.name: Arabinogalactan
e409.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US-authorised additive only, never added to EU Annex II).

e419.name: Gum ghatti
e419.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (Codex/JECFA additive not adopted into EU Annex II).

e424.name: Curdlan
e424.notes: Authorised in the EU since 2007 as a thickener/stabiliser; EFSA re-evaluated it in 2024 with no safety concerns identified at proposed uses.

e427.name: Cassia gum
e427.notes: Authorised in the EU since 2010 as a stabiliser/thickener.

e430.name: Polyoxyethylene (8) stearate
e430.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US/JECFA additive not adopted into EU Annex II).

e434.name: Polyoxyethylene sorbitan monopalmitate (Polysorbate 40)
e434.notes: Authorised EU emulsifier, part of the polysorbates group E432-E436.

e435.name: Polyoxyethylene sorbitan monostearate (Polysorbate 60)
e435.notes: Authorised EU emulsifier/stabiliser, part of the polysorbates group E432-E436.

e436.name: Polyoxyethylene sorbitan tristearate (Polysorbate 65)
e436.notes: Authorised EU emulsifier/stabiliser, part of the polysorbates group E432-E436.

e440a.name: Pectin (incl. sodium/potassium/ammonium pectate)
e440a.notes: Covers pectin E440(i), sodium pectate E440(ii), potassium pectate E440(iii) and ammonium pectate E440(iv) per Commission Regulation (EU) No 231/2012 specifications — all authorised as one 'pectins' entry (E440) in EU Annex II.

e440b.name: Amidated pectin (pectin amide)
e440b.notes: Authorised as E440(ii)/E440b amidated pectin under the EU pectins entry.

e441.name: Superglycerinated hydrogenated rapeseed oil
e441.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (not adopted into EU Annex II).

e442.name: Ammonium phosphatides
e442.notes: Authorised EU emulsifier, commonly used in chocolate/cocoa products.

e443.name: Brominated vegetable oil
e443.notes: Never authorised as a food additive in the EU; not part of the Union list under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

e444.name: Sucrose acetate isobutyrate
e444.notes: Authorised only for specific food categories (notably flavoured/non-alcoholic and alcoholic beverages) per Annex II conditions of use, not for general use.

e445.name: Glycerol esters of wood rosin
e445.notes: Authorised, but restricted to specific categories (chiefly flavoured drinks) under Annex II conditions of use.

e446.name: Succistearin
e446.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e457.name: Alpha-cyclodextrin
e457.notes: Not authorised as an EU food additive (only beta-cyclodextrin, E459, is authorised in the EU).

e458.name: Gamma-cyclodextrin
e458.notes: Not authorised as an EU food additive (only beta-cyclodextrin, E459, is authorised in the EU).

e467.name: Ethulose (ethyl hydroxyethyl cellulose)
e467.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (not adopted into EU Annex II).

e470.name: Sodium/potassium/calcium and magnesium salts of fatty acids
e470.notes: Generic grouping; the EU Union list authorises these specifically as E470a (sodium/potassium/calcium salts) and E470b (magnesium salts) — both approved.

e470a.name: Sodium/potassium/calcium salts of fatty acids
e470a.notes: Authorised EU emulsifier/stabiliser (covers sub-variants sodium, potassium, and calcium salts).

e472d.name: Tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids

e472f.name: Mixed acetic and tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids

e472g.name: Succinylated monoglycerides
e472g.notes: Has an assigned E-number but is not currently approved/authorised for use as a food additive in the EU.

e478.name: Lactylated fatty acid esters of glycerol and propane-1,2-diol
e478.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US/Codex additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e479b.name: Thermally oxidised soya bean oil interacted with mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids
e479b.notes: Authorised but restricted to specific use (professional production of frying oils/fats) under Annex II conditions.

e483.name: Stearyl tartrate
e483.notes: Removed from the EU Union list of authorised food additives by Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/2379 (29 Sept 2023), effective 23 April 2024, because EFSA could not confirm its safety (insufficient toxicological/genotoxicity data). Foods placed on the market before that date could continue to be sold until their minimum durability/use-by date. Note: the UK's retained-EU register still lists it as authorised (GB has not mirrored this EU removal), so this is an EU/GB divergence — this classification reflects EU law only.

e484.name: Stearyl citrate
e484.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (not adopted into EU Annex II; not found in the UK retained-EU register either).

e485.name: Sodium stearoyl fumarate
e485.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e486.name: Calcium stearoyl fumarate
e486.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e487.name: Sodium dodecyl sulfate (sodium lauryl sulfate)
e487.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (not adopted into EU Annex II); used in cosmetics but not permitted as a food additive.

e488.name: Ethoxylated mono- and di-glycerides
e488.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e489.name: Methyl glucoside-coconut oil ester
e489.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (US additive, not adopted into EU Annex II).

e490.name: Propylene glycol
e490.notes: 'E490' specifically is not a valid EU additive number/entry. Propylene glycol itself IS authorised in the EU, but under the different number E1520 (and only for specific uses, e.g. as a carrier/solvent for flavourings and specific food categories). Do not confuse: this entry (E490) as such is not on the EU Union list.

e496.name: Sorbitan trioleate
e496.notes: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (only E491-E495 sorbitan esters are on the Union list); the only EU regulatory hit found for sorbitan trioleate is a food-contact-material (packaging) authorisation, not a food-additive authorisation.

e502.name: Carbonates
e502.notes: Generic OFF taxonomy label for carbonate salts; cross-checked EU-focused additive databases (compareadditives.com) describe E502 as authorised in the EU with compositional/purity specs, used as acidity regulator/raising agent quantum satis. Could not verify exact identity split vs E503 (ammonium carbonates) on EUR-Lex directly due to document size; treat with some caution.

e505.name: Ferrous carbonate
e505.notes: Described by additive databases as an authorised EU food additive with identity/purity criteria set in law; functions as acidity regulator/anti-caking agent and iron source. Not independently confirmed against EUR-Lex Annex II full text (fetch of the consolidated regulation was too large to search reliably), so confidence is medium.

e511.name: Magnesium chloride
e511.notes: Confirmed approved in the EU; used as firming agent/coagulant (e.g. tofu) and stabiliser, generally quantum satis.

e512.name: Stannous chloride
e512.notes: Approved in the EU but restricted to specific uses as an antioxidant, notably canned/bottled white asparagus and certain canned/bottled fruit and vegetables, subject to maximum tin levels.

e513.name: Sulphuric acid
e513.notes: Approved EU food additive, acidity regulator, generally quantum satis. Subject to 2018 EFSA re-evaluation alongside its sodium/potassium/calcium/ammonium salts (E513, E514, E515, E516, E517) with no new safety concerns changing authorisation.

e514ii.name: Sodium hydrogen sulphate
e514ii.notes: Part of the E514 sodium sulphates group, approved EU food additive, acidity regulator, quantum satis in most categories. Re-evaluated by EFSA in 2018 with no safety concerns.

e514i.name: Sodium sulphate
e514i.notes: Approved EU food additive within the E514 sodium sulphates group, quantum satis acidity regulator.

e514.name: Sodium sulphates
e514.notes: Generic taxonomy node covering E514(i) sodium sulphate and E514(ii) sodium hydrogen sulphate, both approved.

e515i.name: Potassium sulphate
e515i.notes: Approved EU food additive within the E515 potassium sulphates group, quantum satis.

e515.name: Potassium sulphates
e515.notes: Generic taxonomy node covering E515(i) potassium sulphate and E515(ii) potassium hydrogen sulphate, both approved.

e515ii.name: Potassium hydrogen sulphate
e515ii.notes: Approved EU food additive within the E515 potassium sulphates group, quantum satis.

e517.name: Ammonium sulphate
e517.notes: Approved EU food additive, quantum satis (notably used in bread-making). Covered by the 2018 EFSA re-evaluation of sulphuric acid and its salts (E513-517).

e518.name: Magnesium sulphate
e518.notes: Approved EU food additive, firming agent/nutrient source, generally quantum satis.

e519.name: Copper sulphate
e519.notes: Not part of the EU Union list of authorised food additives (Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008); it is not a general-use approved food additive in the EU. Search results note it is prohibited/not authorised for food use in the EU, UK, Norway and EAEU. Could not confirm a specific EU decision date, so bannedSince omitted.

e520.name: Aluminium sulphate
e520.notes: Approved but subject to EU aluminium exposure-limit restrictions (Regulation (EU) No 380/2012 amending Annex II, effective from 1 Feb 2014), limiting use to specific food categories (e.g. crystallised/candied fruit, cocktail cherries) with maximum aluminium levels.

e521.name: Aluminium sodium sulphate
e521.notes: Approved but restricted under the same 2014 EU aluminium exposure-limit regime as E520/E522/E523 and E541, limited to specific food categories with maximum aluminium levels.

e522.name: Aluminium potassium sulphate
e522.notes: Approved but restricted under the 2014 EU aluminium exposure-limit regime, limited to specific food categories with maximum aluminium levels.

e523.name: Aluminium ammonium sulphate
e523.notes: Approved but restricted under the 2014 EU aluminium exposure-limit regime, limited to specific food categories with maximum aluminium levels.

e527.name: Ammonium hydroxide
e527.notes: Approved EU food additive, acidity regulator, quantum satis in most food categories.

e528.name: Magnesium hydroxide
e528.notes: Approved EU food additive, acidity regulator, quantum satis.

e529.name: Calcium oxide
e529.notes: Approved EU food additive, acidity regulator, quantum satis.

e530.name: Magnesium oxide
e530.notes: Approved EU food additive, anti-caking agent, quantum satis.

e535.name: Sodium ferrocyanide
e535.notes: Authorised solely as an anti-caking agent in salt and salt substitutes, at a maximum level of 20 mg/kg (expressed as ferrocyanide ion), per the EFSA 2018 re-evaluation and Annex II conditions of use.

e536.name: Potassium ferrocyanide
e536.notes: Authorised solely as an anti-caking agent in salt and salt substitutes, at a maximum level of 20 mg/kg (expressed as ferrocyanide ion).

e538.name: Calcium ferrocyanide
e538.notes: Authorised solely as an anti-caking agent in salt and salt substitutes, at a maximum level of 20 mg/kg (expressed as ferrocyanide ion).

e540.name: Dicalcium diphosphate
e540.notes: Approved EU food additive, raising agent/emulsifying salt, quantum satis in most categories.

e541ii.name: Alkaline sodium aluminium phosphate
e541ii.notes: Approved but restricted under the EU aluminium exposure-limit regime (2014), limited to specific bakery/processed cheese categories with maximum aluminium levels, per EFSA re-evaluation of E541 alongside aluminium sulphates E520-523.

e541i.name: Acid sodium aluminium phosphate
e541i.notes: Approved but restricted under the EU aluminium exposure-limit regime (2014), limited to specific food categories with maximum aluminium levels.

e541.name: Sodium aluminium phosphate
e541.notes: Generic node covering E541(i) and E541(ii); approved but restricted under the 2014 EU aluminium exposure-limit regime.

e542.name: Bone phosphate
e542.notes: Authorised in the EU but only for a limited category of uses (primarily food supplements), not for general food use. EFSA considers it of no safety concern at current authorised use levels.

e543.name: Calcium sodium polyphosphate
e543.notes: Source taxonomy label was a generic placeholder ('E543 food additive') with no name; cross-referenced as calcium sodium polyphosphate, described by secondary sources as approved for use as emulsifier/stabiliser restricted to processed cheese and frozen bakery products. Could not verify directly against EUR-Lex Annex II; confidence limited.

e544.name: Calcium polyphosphate
e544.notes: Approved EU food additive, emulsifier/stabiliser, restricted to certain categories (e.g. processed cheese) rather than quantum satis general use.

e550.name: Sodium silicate
e550.notes: Approved EU food additive within the silicates group (E550-E560), general anti-caking agent, quantum satis in most categories.

e553.name: Magnesium silicates
e553.notes: Covers E553a(i) magnesium silicate, E553a(ii) magnesium trisilicate and E553b talc; approved EU food additives per the EFSA 2018 re-evaluation, restricted to specific food categories with defined maximum levels rather than fully quantum satis.

e554.name: Sodium aluminium silicate
e554.notes: Approved but restricted under the EU aluminium exposure-limit regime, re-evaluated by EFSA in 2020 alongside E555; limited to specific food categories with maximum aluminium levels.

e555.name: Potassium aluminium silicate
e555.notes: Approved but restricted under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 to specific food categories with maximum permitted levels, subject to EU aluminium exposure limits; re-evaluated by EFSA in 2020 alongside E554.

e556.name: Calcium aluminium silicate
e556.notes: Approved for specific applications (salt and substitutes, powdered sugar/dextrose, powdered whey, sliced cheese, spices, tray/pan release), subject to EU aluminium exposure-limit considerations. Confirmed present in EUR-Lex Annex II text directly.

e558.name: Bentonite
e558.notes: Confirmed present as an authorised EU food additive (anti-caking/clarifying agent) directly in the EUR-Lex consolidated Annex II text.

e559.name: Aluminium silicate (kaolin)
e559.notes: Approved but restricted under the EU aluminium exposure-limit regime, limited to specific food categories (e.g. food supplements) with maximum levels.

e560.name: Potassium silicate
e560.notes: Listed within the EU silicates group (E550-E560) as an anti-caking agent; specific conditions of use not independently verified against EUR-Lex, so treat as medium confidence.

e572.name: Magnesium stearate
e572.notes: Approved EU food additive, anti-caking agent/emulsifier, quantum satis in most food categories (also very widely used as an excipient in supplements).

e573.name: Aluminium stearate
e573.notes: Authorised EU food additive with defined identity/purity specifications, but as an aluminium-containing additive it falls under EU aluminium exposure-limit considerations; use appears limited to specific categories rather than general/quantum satis.

e574.name: Gluconic acid
e574.notes: Approved EU food additive, acidity regulator/sequestrant, quantum satis in most food categories.

e576.name: Sodium gluconate
e576.notes: Approved EU food additive, sequestrant/stabiliser/thickener, quantum satis in most food categories.

e577.name: Potassium gluconate
e577.notes: Approved EU food additive, sequestrant, quantum satis in most food categories.

e578.name: Calcium gluconate
e578.notes: Approved EU food additive, sequestrant/firming agent, quantum satis in most food categories.

e579.name: Iron gluconate (ferrous gluconate)
e579.notes: Approved but restricted: authorised specifically as a colour fixative for the blackening/darkening of black (ripe) olives and related olive products, subject to a maximum iron level (150 mg Fe/kg), not a general-use additive.

e585.name: Ferrous lactate
e585.notes: Approved but restricted: authorised specifically for the blackening of green (ripe) olives, subject to a maximum iron level of 150 mg Fe/kg food, not a general-use additive.

e586.name: 4-Hexylresorcinol
e586.notes: Approved but restricted: authorised solely as an antioxidant to prevent melanosis (black spot) in fresh, frozen and deep-frozen crustaceans, subject to a maximum residue level of 2 mg/kg in crustacean meat. Re-evaluated by EFSA in 2014 with no new safety concerns.

e622.name: Monopotassium glutamate
e622.notes: Part of the glutamate group (E620-625), authorized quantum satis (max 10 g/kg expressed as glutamic acid, individually or in combination) across ~67 food categories (e.g. seasonings, condiments, salt substitutes, snacks). Not permitted in foods for infants/young children.

e623.name: Calcium diglutamate
e623.notes: Part of the glutamate group (E620-625); same conditions as E622 (QS, max 10 g/kg as glutamic acid, not for infant food).

e624.name: Monoammonium glutamate
e624.notes: Part of the glutamate group (E620-625); same conditions as E622 (QS, max 10 g/kg as glutamic acid, not for infant food).

e625.name: Magnesium diglutamate
e625.notes: Part of the glutamate group (E620-625); same conditions as E622 (QS, max 10 g/kg as glutamic acid, not for infant food).

e626.name: Guanylic acid
e626.notes: Part of the ribonucleotide flavour-enhancer group (E626-635), authorized in seasonings, snacks, sauces etc. at quantum satis / group limits; not permitted in foods for infants/young children.

e628.name: Dipotassium guanylate
e628.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626/E627 (not for infant food).

e629.name: Calcium guanylate
e629.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626 (not for infant food).

e630.name: Inosinic acid
e630.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626 (not for infant food).

e632.name: Dipotassium inosinate
e632.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626 (not for infant food).

e633.name: Calcium inosinate
e633.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626 (not for infant food).

e634.name: Calcium 5'-ribonucleotide
e634.notes: Part of the E626-635 ribonucleotide group; same general conditions as E626 (not for infant food).

e636.name: Maltol
e636.notes: Authorized flavour enhancer under Annex II, subject to category-specific maximum levels; not permitted in unprocessed foods generally.

e637.name: Ethyl maltol
e637.notes: Authorized flavour enhancer under Annex II, subject to category-specific maximum levels (used widely in confectionery, beverages, bakery). One secondary aggregator claimed non-EU-approval, but this conflicts with multiple other sources; recommend spot-checking the EC additives database if this needs the highest confidence.

e639.name: DL-Alanine
e639.notes: Added to Annex II as a flavour enhancer / carrier for table-top sweeteners by Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/649 (24 April 2015), alongside glycine (E640) and L-leucine (E641). Source list's synonym 'E369' appears to be an Open Food Facts data artifact/typo, not an EU designation.

e640.name: Glycine and its sodium salt
e640.notes: Authorized flavour enhancer added/confirmed via Regulation (EU) 2015/649 grouping with E639/E641; no special mandatory warning label. Covers both E640(i) Glycine and E640(ii) Sodium glycinate.

e641.name: L-Leucine
e641.notes: Added to Annex II by Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/649 of 24 April 2015; authorized as a flavour enhancer and as a tabletting aid/carrier in table-top sweeteners.

e650.name: Zinc acetate
e650.notes: Authorized only in chewing gum, at a maximum level (commonly cited as up to 1000 mg/kg); not permitted in general foods. In use in the EU since 2002.

e710.name: Spiramycin
e710.notes: Macrolide antibiotic; not part of the EU Union list of authorized food additives. Antimicrobial growth promoters including spiramycin were banned as feed additives in the EU from 2006 due to antimicrobial-resistance concerns, and it has never been an authorized food additive.

e713.name: Tylosin
e713.notes: Macrolide antibiotic; not part of the EU Union list of authorized food additives. Antimicrobial growth promoters including tylosin were banned as feed additives in the EU from 2006 due to antimicrobial-resistance concerns, and it has never been an authorized food additive.

e900a.name: Dimethyl polysiloxane
e900a.notes: This is the substance authorized in Annex II simply as 'E900 Dimethyl polysiloxane' (antifoaming agent), permitted only in specific food categories (e.g. fats/oils for frying, jams, beverages) at defined maximum levels, not general use.

e900b.name: Methylphenylpolysiloxane
e900b.notes: Only dimethyl polysiloxane is listed in Annex II under E900; methylphenylpolysiloxane (a related but distinct silicone) does not appear as an authorized Annex II entry.

e902.name: Candelilla wax
e902.notes: Authorized as a glazing agent only in specific food categories (e.g. confectionery, chewing gum, surface treatment of certain fruits) at quantum satis within those categories, not general use.

e905.name: Synthetic wax (superseded generic name)
e905.notes: The generic 'synthetic wax' designation is not itself an Annex II entry; the current Annex II entry under E905 covers specifically 'microcrystalline wax' (see E905c(i)). The broader synthetic-wax/petroleum-wax family was narrowed by Commission Regulation (EU) No 1147/2012.

e905a.name: Mineral oil (food grade)
e905a.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry; the EU narrowed permitted E905 uses to specific microcrystalline wax only (Reg (EU) 1147/2012). Generic food-grade mineral oil as a stand-alone additive is not on the Union list.

e905b.name: Petroleum jelly
e905b.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e905c.name: Petroleum wax (generic)
e905c.notes: The generic 'petroleum wax' parent entry is not itself authorized; only the specific microcrystalline wax sub-form (commonly labelled E905c(i)) is on the Union list. See separate entries for E905ci and E905cii below.

e905ci.name: Microcrystalline wax
e905ci.notes: This is the actual EU Annex II entry (listed simply as 'E905 Microcrystalline wax'). Authorized only as a surface-treatment/glazing agent on specific items: non-chocolate confectionery, chewing gum, decorations/coatings/fillings (except fruit-based fillings), and surface treatment of melon, papaya, mango and avocado. Must meet purity criteria (e.g. max 50 microg/kg benzo[a]pyrene) under Regulation (EU) No 231/2012.

e905cii.name: Paraffin wax
e905cii.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry; only microcrystalline wax (E905c(i)) remains authorized after Regulation (EU) No 1147/2012 narrowed the E905 group.

e905d.name: Mineral oil, high viscosity
e905d.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e905e.name: Mineral oil, medium viscosity
e905e.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e905f.name: Mineral oil, medium and low viscosity class II
e905f.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e905g.name: Mineral oil, medium and low viscosity class III
e905g.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e906.name: Benzoin resin (gum benzoin)
e906.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry in the current EU food additives regulation.

e907.name: Hydrogenated poly-1-decene
e907.notes: Authorized glazing agent, used mainly in chewing gum, subject to a specified ADI (20 mg/kg bw/day) and category-specific conditions.

e908.name: Rice bran wax
e908.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry in the current EU food additives regulation.

e909.name: Spermaceti
e909.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry; sperm-whale-derived wax, long out of use and not on the current EU Union list.

e910.name: Wax ester
e910.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e911.name: Fatty acid methyl esters
e911.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e912.name: Montanic acid esters
e912.notes: Removed from the Union list of authorized food additives by Commission Regulation (EU) No 957/2014 of 10 September 2014, effective 1 October 2014, due to insufficient toxicological data to complete EFSA's re-evaluation.

e913.name: Lanolin
e913.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry for food use.

e914.name: Oxidised polyethylene wax
e914.notes: Authorized glazing agent under Annex II, limited to specific applications (chiefly chewing gum) at defined maximum levels.

e915.name: Esters of colophane (colophony)
e915.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry in the current EU food additives regulation.

e916.name: Calcium iodate
e916.notes: Flour/dough bleaching-oxidizing agent; not authorized in the EU. Chemical flour bleaching agents generally have been prohibited across the EU.

e917.name: Potassium iodate
e917.notes: Flour/dough bleaching-oxidizing agent; not authorized in the EU.

e918.name: Nitrogen oxides
e918.notes: Flour bleaching agent; not authorized in the EU.

e919.name: Nitrosyl chloride
e919.notes: Flour bleaching agent; not authorized in the EU.

e920.name: L-cysteine
e920.notes: Authorized under Annex II as a flour treatment agent/dough conditioner, limited to specific bread/bakery categories and maximum levels; no ADI established.

e921.name: L-cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate
e921.notes: Grouped with E920 in the EU additive specifications (Reg (EU) 231/2012) as a form of L-cysteine; same restricted flour-treatment conditions apply. Not separately itemised as a distinct Annex II entry from E920 in the consolidated text reviewed, so confidence is medium-high rather than definitive.

e922.name: Potassium persulfate
e922.notes: Formerly used as a flour improving agent; no longer authorized for food use in the EU and not found in the current Annex II list.

e923.name: Ammonium persulfate
e923.notes: Same persulfate family as E922; not authorized in the EU, not found in current Annex II list.

e924a.name: Potassium bromate
e924a.notes: Genotoxic-carcinogen concern (IARC 'possibly carcinogenic'). Banned as a flour treatment agent across the EU; the UK banned it domestically from 1 April 1990, and it has never been included in the harmonised EU Union list of authorized food additives. Exact single EU-wide banning-regulation date not confirmed from primary EUR-Lex text; treat 1990 as approximate.

e924b.name: Calcium bromate
e924b.notes: Same bromate family as E924a; not authorized in the EU, treated the same way as potassium bromate (genotoxic carcinogen, flour-treatment ban).

e925.name: Chlorine
e925.notes: Flour bleaching agent; not authorized in the EU.

e926.name: Chlorine dioxide
e926.notes: Flour bleaching agent; not authorized as a food additive in the EU (distinct from its use as a water-treatment/disinfection agent).

e927.name: Azodicarbonamide
e927.notes: Dough conditioner/flour bleaching agent used in the US/Canada but banned as a food additive in the EU (breakdown product semicarbazide is a potential carcinogen concern); not on the Union list.

e927b.name: Carbamide (urea)
e927b.notes: Authorized under Annex II, but only as a stabilizer in the chewing-mass of sugar-free chewing gum, up to 30 g/kg. Not permitted as a flour treatment agent.

e929.name: Acetone peroxide
e929.notes: Flour bleaching agent; banned in the EU (as in Canada, UK, China).

e940.name: Dichlorodifluoromethane
e940.notes: Obsolete CFC propellant; not found in the current Annex II list, and CFCs are separately restricted in the EU on environmental (ozone-layer) grounds.

e941.name: Nitrogen
e941.notes: Authorized packaging gas/propellant, permitted quantum satis.

e942.name: Nitrous oxide
e942.notes: Authorized propellant (e.g. whipped cream aerosols/canisters). Legal food-additive status is unaffected by separate, unrelated EU/member-state restrictions on nitrous oxide sold for recreational inhalant misuse.

e945.name: Chloropentafluoroethane
e945.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry; this is a CFC-related propellant not on the current EU Union list.

e946.name: Octafluorocyclobutane
e946.notes: Not found as an authorized Annex II entry.

e948.name: Oxygen
e948.notes: Authorized quantum satis across all food categories, including for infants and young children, per EFSA's 2025 re-evaluation.

e949.name: Hydrogen
e949.notes: Authorized quantum satis across all food categories, including for infants and young children, per EFSA's 2025 re-evaluation.

e956.name: Alitame
e956.notes: Alitame was never actually authorized in the EU: the sole application (by Danisco) was withdrawn, and it does not appear in the current Annex II sweeteners list, despite some secondary sources incorrectly claiming EU approval alongside Australia/NZ/China/Mexico.

e958.name: Glycyrrhizin (glycyrrhizinic acid / liquorice extract)
e958.notes: Not itself listed as an Annex II food-additive entry (it is regulated as a flavouring/ingredient rather than under the additive numbering), but the EU's Food Information to Consumers framework mandates a specific warning label -- 'Contains liquorice - people suffering from hypertension should avoid excessive consumption' -- once concentrations exceed set thresholds (4 g/kg in confectionery; 50 mg/L in beverages, 300 mg/L for alcoholic beverages >1.2% ABV). Flagging the additive-vs-flavouring nuance for reviewer awareness.

e960a.name: Steviol glycosides from Stevia
e960a.notes: Authorized sweetener under Annex II, with category-specific maximum levels (mg/kg expressed as steviol equivalents) rather than general/QS use.

e960b.name: Rebaudioside from multiple gene donors expressed in Yarrowia lipolytica
e960b.notes: Steviol-glycoside sweetener produced by fermentation, authorized as part of the expanded E960(a-d) group; introduced by amendments following Regulation (EU) 2021/1156 (13 July 2021) and subsequent 2022-2023 amendments to Reg 231/2012/1333/2008, permitted in the same food categories as other steviol glycosides.

e960c.name: Enzymatically produced steviol glycosides
e960c.notes: Authorized via Commission Regulation (EU) 2021/1156 (13 July 2021, in force 3 August 2021, transition ending 2 February 2023), later expanded (Oct 2022) to include additional enzymatically-produced rebaudiosides (M, D, AM) as E960c(i)-(iv). Permitted in the same categories as other steviol glycosides.

e960d.name: Glucosylated steviol glycosides
e960d.notes: Introduced as E960d by amendments to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 and (EU) No 231/2012 in March 2023, and confirmed present in the current consolidated Annex II text (2026) under the steviol-glycosides group E960a-d.

e964.name: Polyglycitol syrup
e964.notes: Authorized sweetener/bulking agent confirmed present in the current consolidated Annex II text.

e1103.name: Invertase
e1103.notes: Unlike the other E110x enzymes, 'E 1103 Invertase' literally appears in the current EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 Annex II Part B, carried over as a legacy authorization from Directive 95/2/EC. The regulation itself states this legacy E-number entry will be repealed once the Union list of food enzymes (under Reg 1332/2008) takes effect - which has not yet happened - so invertase currently retains transitional E-number status. Expect this to move fully to food-enzyme regulation once that list is finalized.

e1105.name: Lysozyme
e1105.notes: Genuine, current EU food additive (confirmed in EUR-Lex consolidated Annex II Part B). Authorised specifically as a preservative in ripened cheese to prevent 'late blowing' defect (Clostridium tyrobutyricum contamination); not a general-purpose additive. Derived from hen egg white - relevant for egg-allergic consumers and subject to allergen labelling.

e1200.name: Polydextrose
e1200.notes: Authorised EU food additive (bulking agent/thickener/stabiliser) at quantum satis in multiple food categories under Annex II Part E to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008, plus Annex III uses; excludes food supplements for infants and young children.

e1201.name: Polyvinylpyrrolidone
e1201.notes: Authorised EU food additive but restricted in scope: permitted as a coating/binder in solid food supplements (tablets/coated tablets) and, since Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/1038, in foods for special medical purposes in tablet/coated tablet form. Not permitted for general food use.

e1202.name: Polyvinylpolypyrrolidone
e1202.notes: Authorised EU food additive, restricted to use as a clarifying/stabilising agent in specific categories (notably wine and other beverage clarification) and solid food supplements per Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Not a general-use additive.

e1203.name: Polyvinyl alcohol
e1203.notes: Authorised EU food additive but restricted to use as a coating/glazing agent for solid food supplements (capsules/tablets); not permitted in general foods.

e1204.name: Pullulan
e1204.notes: Authorised EU food additive under Annex II Part E to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 at quantum satis, but limited to specific categories: 'other confectionery including breath-freshening microsweets' and solid food supplements (excluding those for infants/young children), as a glazing/coating agent.

e1205.name: Basic methacrylate copolymer
e1205.notes: Authorised EU food additive (glazing agent) but restricted solely to solid food supplements and solid foods for special medical purposes (FSMPs), per Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013 amending Annex II. Not for general food use.

e1206.name: Neutral methacrylate copolymer
e1206.notes: Authorised EU food additive (glazing agent), restricted to solid food supplements and solid FSMPs per Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013.

e1207.name: Anionic methacrylate copolymer
e1207.notes: Authorised EU food additive (glazing agent), restricted to solid food supplements and solid FSMPs per Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013.

e1208.name: Polyvinylpyrrolidone-vinyl acetate copolymer
e1208.notes: Authorised EU food additive restricted to use in solid food supplements only.

e1209.name: Polyvinyl alcohol-polyethylene glycol-graft-co-polymer
e1209.notes: Authorised EU food additive with a single permitted use: as a glazing/coating agent for food supplements supplied in solid form, per Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

e1210.name: Carbomer
e1210.notes: Added to the EU list of authorised food additives via an amendment published in the Official Journal around March 2023, restricted to food supplements in solid form. One of the most recently added E-numbers in this family.

e1400.name: Dextrin, roasted starch
e1400.notes: Explicitly excluded from the EU legal definition of 'food additive' by Article 3(2)(a)(vi) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('dextrins, roasted starch...'), meaning it does not require Annex II authorization and can be used freely as a food ingredient. It therefore does not carry a genuine EU E-number under the additives regulation, but is fully permitted for use - not banned.

e1401.name: Acid-treated modified starch
e1401.notes: Excluded from the EU 'food additive' definition by Article 3(2)(a)(vi) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('starch modified by acid... treatment'). Freely usable as food, not subject to Annex II positive-list authorization; no genuine EU E-number.

e1402.name: Alkaline modified starch
e1402.notes: Excluded from the EU 'food additive' definition by Article 3(2)(a)(vi) ('starch modified by... alkali treatment'). Freely usable, no genuine EU E-number required.

e1403.name: Bleached starch
e1403.notes: Explicitly excluded from the EU 'food additive' definition by Article 3(2)(a)(vi) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('bleached starch'). Confirmed present verbatim in the consolidated regulation text. Freely usable, no Annex II authorization needed.

e1404.name: Oxidised starch
e1404.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed listed in Annex II Part B to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Like all modified starches in this family, not permitted in foods for infants and young children (per Article 3(2)/recitals restricting modified starches in these categories).

e1405.name: Enzyme treated starch
e1405.notes: Excluded from the EU 'food additive' definition by Article 3(2)(a)(vi) of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('starch treated by amylolytic enzymes'). Freely usable, no Annex II authorization/E-number required.

e1410.name: Monostarch phosphate
e1410.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1411.name: Distarch glycerol
e1411.notes: Not found anywhere in the current EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 (checked directly, phrase absent). Unlike E1400/1401/1402/1403/1405, this substance is not covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) 'not an additive' exclusion either - as a true cross-linked modified starch it would require Annex II listing to be lawful, and it is not listed. This is a Codex/INS number that was never adopted into the EU Union list. Note: some low-quality secondary sites incorrectly claim it is 'EU authorised' - not corroborated by the primary legal text.

e1412.name: Distarch phosphate
e1412.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1413.name: Phosphated distarch phosphate
e1413.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1414.name: Acetylated distarch phosphate
e1414.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1420.name: Acetylated starch
e1420.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed by direct text search of the consolidated Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('Acetylated starch' entry located, corresponding to E 1420). Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1421.name: Acetylated starch
e1421.notes: Duplicate-name entry in the Open Food Facts taxonomy distinct from E1420 (same common name, different INS/Codex number). Not found anywhere in the EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 and not part of the EU's 12-member authorised modified-starch family (E1404, E1410, E1412-1414, E1420, E1422, E1440, E1442, E1450-1452). Not covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) exclusion either. Treated as not authorised in the EU.

e1422.name: Acetylated distarch adipate
e1422.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1423.name: Acetylated distarch glycerol
e1423.notes: Not found anywhere in the EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 (checked directly, phrase absent) and not among the EU's authorised modified-starch family, nor covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) exclusion. Codex/INS number not adopted by the EU.

e1430.name: Distarch glycerine
e1430.notes: Not found anywhere in the EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 (checked directly, phrase absent). Not part of the EU's authorised modified-starch family and not covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) exclusion. Same underlying substance concept as E1411 (Distarch glycerol) - neither is an EU-recognized E-number.

e1440.name: Hydroxypropyl starch
e1440.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1441.name: Hydroxy propyl distarch glycerine
e1441.notes: Not found anywhere in the EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 and not among the EU's authorised modified-starch family; not covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) exclusion. Not an EU-recognized E-number.

e1442.name: Hydroxypropyl distarch phosphate
e1442.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1443.name: Hydroxy propyl distarch glycerol
e1443.notes: Not found anywhere in the EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 and not among the EU's authorised modified-starch family; not covered by the Article 3(2)(a)(vi) exclusion. Not an EU-recognized E-number (duplicate/variant naming of glycerol-linked starches similar to E1411/E1430).

e1451.name: Acetylated oxidised starch
e1451.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1452.name: Starch aluminium octenyl succinate
e1452.notes: Genuine authorised EU modified starch food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B. Not permitted in foods for infants and young children.

e1505.name: Triethyl citrate
e1505.notes: Genuine authorised EU food additive confirmed present in Annex II Part B of the consolidated Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 ('Triethyl citrate'). Used as a carrier/emulsifier/sequestrant/stabiliser, notably as a foam stabiliser in some beverages.

e1517.name: Glyceryl diacetate
e1517.notes: Authorised EU food additive ('Glyceryl diacetate (diacetin)'), confirmed in Annex II Part B and the UK approved additives list. Restricted to use as a carrier/solvent for flavourings, generally up to 3 g/kg of food.

e1518.name: Glyceryl triacetate
e1518.notes: Authorised EU food additive ('Glycerol triacetate; triacetin'), confirmed in Annex II Part B and the UK approved additives list. Permitted as a carrier for flavourings/colourants and in chewing gum; used within specific category limits rather than for general food use.

e1519.name: Benzyl alcohol
e1519.notes: Authorised EU food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B and the UK approved additives list, and re-evaluated by EFSA. Used as a carrier/solvent in flavouring preparations under specific conditions of use rather than general food use.

e1520.name: Propylene Glycol
e1520.notes: Authorised EU food additive ('Propane-1,2-diol; propylene glycol'), confirmed in Annex II Part B and the UK approved additives list. Permitted in specific food categories (e.g. flavourings, some processed foods, certain confectionery) with maximum levels; not a general-purpose additive.

e1521.name: Polyethylene glycol
e1521.notes: Authorised EU food additive, confirmed in Annex II Part B and the UK approved additives list. Permitted mainly as a glazing/coating agent for food supplements in solid form, per specific Annex II conditions rather than general food use.

tbhq.name: TBHQ (tert-Butylhydroquinone)
tbhq.notes: No E-number assigned. Not authorized in EU food. Common in US snacks, fast food, frozen foods.

bvo.name: Brominated Vegetable Oil (BVO)
bvo.notes: No E-number in EU. Not authorized in EU food. Was used in US citrus sodas. FDA banned it in 2024.

potassium-bromate.name: Potassium Bromate
potassium-bromate.notes: Not authorized in EU. Used in some US commercial bread to strengthen dough. Classified as possibly carcinogenic.

potassium-iodate.name: Potassium Iodate
potassium-iodate.notes: Not authorized in EU food use.

benzoyl-peroxide.name: Benzoyl Peroxide
benzoyl-peroxide.notes: Not authorized for flour treatment in EU food.

calcium-peroxide.name: Calcium Peroxide
calcium-peroxide.notes: Not authorized for flour treatment in EU food.

chlorine-dioxide.name: Chlorine Dioxide
chlorine-dioxide.notes: Not authorized for flour treatment in EU food.

olestra.name: Olestra (Olean)
olestra.notes: Not authorized in EU food. Used in some US fat-free snack products.

recombinant-bgh.name: Recombinant Bovine Growth Hormone (rBGH / rBST)
recombinant-bgh.notes: Not authorized in EU. Used in US dairy to increase milk production. EU bans milk from rBGH-treated cows.

ractopamine.name: Ractopamine
ractopamine.notes: Not authorized in EU. Used in US pork/beef production to promote lean meat growth.
